old_claim.note: Record Keeping is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Qin weapon marks and inscriptions supported tracking, quality control, production accountability, and inventory-like accounting for weapon components.

source_shape.source_locator: The paper says Qin marking practices let objects and people be tracked, quality checked, and numbers marshalled, and discusses marks on bronze weapons and crossbow triggers.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies marking and accountancy as active practices in Qin weapon production and logistics.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Record keeping is not merely a distant historical ancestor here; marked weapons and components directly enabled quality control, tracking, and accountability in the scoped armory system.

ontology_before: State Armories treated Record Keeping as a generic ancient predecessor while relying on a cuneiform-writing source that did not support the armory claim.
ontology_after: State Armories treats Record Keeping as a direct enabling practice for Qin weapon marking, quality control, and logistics.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: state_armories->record_keeping is now an enabling edge with direct source support.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to an unmarked household weapon cache rather than state-controlled weapon production and issue.
A stronger source showed Qin weapon marks were unrelated to tracking, accountancy, quality control, or production accountability.
A more precise weapon-inscription-accounting node replaced record_keeping as the direct prerequisite.
short_reason: Qin weapon markings directly supported production tracking and quality control.
